Software Engineering Job

Programming

A practical guide for Computer Science and Software Engineering students navigating the AI era

12 min read
Just now

--

By Mpaata Solomon | Software Engineering Student, Full Stack Developer & Technology Writer
The question every tech student eventually faces — and the answer that could shape the next decade of their career.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s 11:47 PM……..Your laptop is still open from today’s Software Engineering lecture. The assignment you promised yourself you’d finish can wait another hour — or so you tell yourself. You unlock your phone and start scrolling through LinkedIn. Then two job posts appear. Software Engineer — React, Node.js, Kubernetes Prompt Engineer — Up to $250,000
You stop scrolling.
Suddenly, four years of studying algorithms, databases, operating systems and software engineering feels like it might have been a mistake.
Did I choose the wrong degree?
If you’ve asked yourself that question recently, you’re not alone. This article is for every Computer Science and Software Engineering student trying to make sense of what AI actually means for their career.
Why I Wrote This
As a software engineering student, I’ve watched classmates grow increasingly anxious about AI.
Every week, someone asks a version of the same question.
“Is coding dead?”
“Should I stop learning React and just focus on AI?”
“Will software engineers even exist in ten years?”
I asked the same questions myself. So I spent weeks reading hiring reports, engineering blogs, developer surveys and industry research before writing this.
What I found was not what the headlines suggested. Here is what the data, the engineers and the companies actually say.

A Story That Could Be Yours
Imagine two software engineering students graduating from the same university.
One spends most of their time chasing every new AI trend, convinced that prompt engineering alone will secure a strong career.
The other builds a solid foundation in programming, algorithms, databases, Git, cloud computing and software architecture. They also use AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude and GitHub Copilot — not as a replacement for learning, but as tools that make them more productive.
A few years later, both are working with AI. The difference is that the second graduate isn’t just using AI — they’re building applications powered by it. Their engineering foundation allows them to understand, customize, debug and improve AI systems in ways the first graduate simply cannot.
The lesson isn’t that prompt engineering has no value. It’s that AI becomes far more powerful in the hands of someone who already understands how software is designed and built.

The Numbers — Because Opinions Are Cheap
According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (2023), software developer employment is projected to grow 25% between 2022 and 2032 — one of the fastest-growing occupations tracked across all industries.
The Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2023, covering over 90,000 developers globally, found that software engineers remain the most hired and highest-compensated technical professionals across every region surveyed.
The World Economic Forum Future of Jobs Report 2023 listed software developers among the top five roles expected to see the highest absolute demand growth through 2027 — even after accounting for AI-driven automation.
The GitHub Octoverse 2023 report showed the number of developers on GitHub crossed 100 million, with more new contributors joining than in any previous year.
These are not opinions. They tell a consistent story.

Key statistics from BLS, Stack Overflow, WEF and GitHub showing sustained growth in software engineering demand.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

BLS, Stack Overflow, WEF and GitHub
Common Myths — Cleared Up
❌ Myth: AI will replace programmers.
✅ Reality: AI is changing how programming is done, not eliminating the need for it. Tools like GitHub Copilot make engineers faster — they don’t make engineers unnecessary.

❌ Myth: Prompt engineering requires no technical knowledge.
✅ Reality: The most effective prompts come from people who understand the domain deeply. A software engineer can immediately evaluate whether AI-generated code is correct. Someone without that foundation often cannot.

❌ Myth: Companies only want AI skills now.
✅ Reality: Companies want engineers who combine solid software fundamentals with AI literacy. AI skills on top of an empty foundation are far less valuable than AI skills built on top of strong engineering experience.

❌ Myth: Prompt engineering is a long-term career equivalent to software engineering.
✅ Reality: Prompt engineering is becoming a capability expected across many professions — the way knowing Excel became expected of office workers. It is a skill within careers, not a standalone career in itself.

❌ Myth: Learning to code is no longer worth the effort.
✅ Reality: This claim has surfaced in every decade since the 1980s — through visual programming tools, drag-and-drop platforms and low-code builders. Each time, it proved wrong.

What Is a Software Engineer?
A software engineer designs, builds, tests and maintains the systems that power the modern world. Every app, every website, every bank transaction, every stream you watch — all of it exists because software engineers built and continue to maintain the underlying systems.
The role spans many specialisations — frontend, backend, full stack, DevOps, data engineering, machine learning engineering — but they all share a common foundation: the ability to reason about complex systems, write reliable code and solve problems in context.
Engineering doesn’t just teach you to code. It teaches you to think in trade-offs, reason through systems and approach problems in a way that applies far beyond any single language or framework.

What Is a Prompt Engineer?
Imagine hiring an incredibly intelligent intern. They can write, analyse, summarise and generate code. But they need clear, precise instructions to do any of these well. Structured guidance produces outstanding results. Vague instructions produce poor ones.
That is essentially what prompt engineering involves — designing and refining instructions given to AI models to produce accurate, consistent outputs.
It is a genuinely valuable skill. Anthropic listed a Prompt Engineer role at between $250,000 and $335,000, which understandably set the industry talking.
But here is the question that salary figure doesn’t answer: is it a career, or is it a capability that sits inside other careers?
That distinction is the heart of this article.

How These Two Roles Actually Relate
Most articles frame this as a competition. It isn’t.
Software Engineering Foundations
↓
Computer Science Thinking
↓
AI Literacy & Prompt Skills
↓
Products & Systems
↓
Businesses
↓
UsersSoftware engineering is the foundation on which everything else is built — including AI products. Prompt engineering sits within that stack as a valuable tool, not as an alternative to it.

How software engineering, AI literacy and prompt skills relate within the broader product development stack.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Code. Understand AI. Prompt with purpose. Build the future. 💻🤖
The Comparison Every Student Needs to See
Software Engineer

• Builds products, systems and infrastructure

• Works in every industry on earth

• Millions of job openings globally

• Clear growth path: Junior → Senior → Staff → Principal

• Can build AI products independently

• Excellent freelance potential

• Career established for over five decades
Prompt Engineer

• Designs and optimises AI instructions

• Primarily AI-focused roles

• Limited and evolving job openings

• Career growth path less clearly defined

• Cannot usually build AI products alone

• Moderate freelance potential

• Emerging field, still being defined

What the Industry Is Actually Saying
Andrej Karpathy, Former Director of AI at Tesla and Co-founder of OpenAI, once said that the hottest new programming language is English — a statement widely misread as a declaration that coding was finished. His actual point was about AI accessibility. He has continued to teach neural networks from first principles and advocates for deep engineering as the basis for serious AI work.
At Google I/O 2023, Sundar Pichai emphasised AI as a tool to help developers build software more efficiently, while Google continued investing heavily in engineering talent across its infrastructure and product teams.
Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, has consistently encouraged people to learn programming, arguing that understanding how software works remains genuinely valuable even as AI becomes more capable — because the people who understand the foundations are best positioned to direct and improve these systems.
A senior engineering manager at Google, as reported by The Information in 2023, put it plainly:
“We still need people who understand systems deeply. Prompt engineering is a feature of the job now, not the job itself.”
Perspectives from Andrej Karpathy, Sundar Pichai and Sam Altman on software engineering in the age of AI.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Industry leaders agree: AI is changing software engineering — not replacing it.
What Companies Are Actually Building
Spotify expanded its engineering teams when it introduced AI-powered features like AI DJ and personalised recommendations — because more AI features meant more infrastructure work, not less.
Netflix serves over 260 million subscribers across 190 countries simultaneously. Its engineering blog details how hundreds of software engineers maintain the systems that make this possible. Reliability at that scale cannot be replicated by prompting alone.
OpenAI itself is the clearest example. ChatGPT — the tool that made prompt engineering a household term — was built by thousands of software engineers working on infrastructure, training pipelines, safety systems and deployment architecture. The tool that some say makes coding unnecessary was built entirely by people who could code.

The Skills Engineering Builds That AI Doesn’t Replace
Systems Thinking
Designing how a complex system should be structured requires technical depth and precise understanding of business context. Engineering teaches you to think this way. Prompting alone does not.
Security and Adversarial Reasoning
Identifying vulnerabilities and reasoning about how systems can fail under attack requires a kind of thinking that current AI handles poorly in nuanced, high-stakes situations.
Product Thinking and Requirements Analysis
Understanding what a user actually needs — as opposed to what they say they need — combines empathy, communication and engineering judgment in ways AI currently approximates but does not reliably replicate.
Leadership and Trade-off Analysis
Senior engineers spend much of their time deciding between competing priorities — speed versus reliability, cost versus performance. These decisions ripple across organisations for years and require judgment built through sustained experience.

Four skill areas built through engineering experience that AI systems cannot yet reliably replicate.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The most four skills in need.
What Hiring Managers Actually Look For
Based on published accounts from engineering leads at Google, Microsoft, Stripe and Shopify, here is what technical hiring managers consistently prioritise.
A GitHub profile with real activity — Commits, projects and growth over time. Hiring managers read code.
At least one substantial project — Something designed, built, deployed and debugged by you. Not a tutorial. Something real.
Internship or work experience — Even a short placement demonstrates professional teamwork and real delivery.
Demonstrated problem-solving — The ability to reason through an unfamiliar problem and think through trade-offs clearly.
Communication and teamwork — Software
